title: Furnish a Room
summary: Five pieces that add up to a room you built — from a weekend shelf to a dining table.
description:
This one is a long game, and it is honest about that.

These are not weekend projects after the first two. A platform bed is a lot of wood and a lot of glue-ups; a dining table is the most demanding flat surface most people ever make. Together they are a year of weekends, and the point of ordering them is that each piece teaches the skill the next one assumes you already have.

The scale grows on purpose. A wobbly bookshelf is annoying. A wobbly dining table is a piece of furniture that everyone you know eats at, notices, and is too polite to mention.

steps.reason: Casework, cheaply. Sides, shelves, a back that keeps it square — the same anatomy as every cabinet you will ever build, in pine, where a mistake costs almost nothing.

The first piece where the WOOD is the point. Walnut is expensive and unforgiving of tear-out, and a floating shelf has no visible support to hide behind — it is your first exercise in making the thing look effortless.
Casework plus a moving part. A drawer that binds is a drawer you fight every night. This is the step that takes you from building boxes to building furniture people use without thinking about it.

Scale. Nothing here is technically harder than the nightstand, but everything is bigger, heavier, and has to be assembled in a bedroom rather than a shop. Managing large stock and knock-down joinery is its own skill, and this is where you learn it.

Last, and hardest. A wide, flat, glued-up top that stays flat through every season, on a base that survives being leaned on. It asks for everything the other four taught you, and it is the piece people will still be eating at long after you have stopped noticing the mistakes.
